title: CEOs in Their 60s Are New Norm With Companies Picking Older Bosses
title_original: CEOs in Their 60s Are New Norm With Companies Picking Older Bosses
summary: This graying of the corner office, shown in a National Bureau of Economic Research working paper that analyzed the hiring and career patterns of more than 50,500 CEOs in the US between 2000 and 2023, reflects “fundamental changes” in how executives build careers and how companies select their leaders, according to the study’s authors, with varied business experience favored over raw ability. “As executives require longer career paths to build such diverse capabilities, firms appoint older CEOs.” The trend is most pronounced among smaller, privately-held firms, the researchers said. That’s because larger companies can offer internal candidates a wider range of assignments, cultivating a well-roundedness that smaller companies “can only access by hiring executives who accumulated that experience by moving across many firms and industries.” (This also helps explain why the average age of CEOs at S&P 500 Index companies is below the average and not advancing as fast, rising from roughly 56 years old in 2000 to 58.5 in 2023.) Executives with experience in the strategy consulting sector — which “fast-tracks the accumulation of generalist skills,” the researchers note — have grabbed a greater share of CEO roles , according to a separate analysis by Live Data Technologies for Bloomberg.

title: Claim Severity Trends for Medical Malpractice ‘Stand Out’: S&P GMI

Medical professional liability occurrence reached $151,768 per open claim in accident year 2018, which represents the highest severity figure across all casualty insurance lines before moderating in more recent years, a typical trend for newer claims where cost pressures continue to emerge and mature. title: Global Financial Watchdog to Share Insights on Anthropic’s Mythos
title_original: Global Financial Watchdog to Share Insights on Anthropic’s Mythos
summary: The Financial Stability Board is gathering information from members about potential risks posed by Anthropic’s Mythos model as it look to share such insights more broadly among its network of regulators and central bankers to help them judge the risks of autonomous cyber attacks. It was discussed at a meeting of FSB representatives on Wednesday amid concerns that financial systems beyond the US are disadvantaged because they have little access to the model created by San Francisco-based Anthropic. Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem, who heads the FSB’s key committee for monitoring risks, said officials have “work to do” as they assess the severity of the risks posed by the artificial intelligence model relative to other budding dangers like private credit and the global energy crisis.

title: GE Vernova Must Continue Work on $4.5B Wind Farm Project, Judge Rules

summary: A Massachusetts judge on Friday blocked GE Vernova from abandoning work on the largest offshore wind farm in New England after developer Vineyard Wind refused to pay over $300 million the turbine supplier says it is owed for the $4.5 billion project. A spokesperson for Vineyard Wind, a joint venture between Spain’s Iberdrola IBE.MC and Denmark’s Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners, welcomed the judge’s ruling, saying in a statement that it ensures it “can continue delivering much needed energy to Massachusetts customers and the New England grid.” Cambridge, Massachusetts-based GE Vernova said it will “remain engaged in supporting the safe operation of the project while we evaluate next steps.” Vineyard Wind contends it is owed the money as a result of that blade failure, which caused two years of delays after the manufacturing flaw that led to the failure was found to be widespread, requiring other blades to be replaced. Suffolk County Superior Court Judge Peter Krupp in Boston issued a preliminary injunction at developer Vineyard Wind’s request preventing a GE Vernova unit from terminating their contract and ceasing work effective April 28.
